Question number 19 came from Phillip who wanted to know how to improve his process for creating information products. Kind of a tough question to answer since I don't really know what Phillip's process is but there's some things to think about in that area and believe it or not, I'm constantly amazed. In fact I was talking to a friend of mine yesterday whose name you'd know, he's a big internet guru and we were just kind of chuckling about how when we share ideas with people either in conferences or webinars like this quite often, the people we're talking to will be just blown away by what we consider to be a very simple idea. In this particular area, I mean like for example, I was talking to a group the other day and told them that business people and told them that the number one, the most important thing they need to do is to keep a list of their customers. There were several people who were just blown away by that advice. It was just absolutely brilliant. It's a record of who buys from you and what. But anyway, the last time I talked to somebody about information publishing, it happened to be Paris Lampropolis who most of you know is a high six figure copyright. In fact I think Paris easily does over a million dollars a year in royalties and Paris was telling me about his first experience in this particular area and he made some of the classical blunders so we'll talk about those in a minute. The key questions you have to answer if you're going to get into the information publishing industry is to first of all identify your niche. You need to find an area where you believe that you can bring tremendous value to people. It's as determined for us by a client for example. But obviously your first question is what am I going to write about? Once you've determined that then the next question is who's the best prospect for this kind of a report? Let's assume it's a report at this point. We'll talk a little bit about format in a minute. Who's my best prospect? Where will I find them? Will I find them on a direct mail list or an e-list? Will I find them as subscribers to an online easing? Are they registrants to a membership website? Where are these guys online? If you're anything like me you probably hate the whole idea of pay per click and SEO search engine optimization as a way to acquire prospects because they're so passive. You bid on a keyword but then you have to wait for somebody to type that keyword in or if you're doing search engine optimization it's the same thing. You have to wait for someone else to decide that they want what it is you're selling or talking about. It's just too passive for me anyway. So much of the control over your marketing is lost as a result of that. I would want to find a product area where there are mailing lists available or there are websites or easings that I could partner with that would give me more control over the outbound messaging where I could proactively go to prospects rather than wait for them to discover that they need me. So get a feel for where your prospects are hiding and then the most cost efficient way to reach that prospect. For a lot of us that will be online because online is so cheap. But again I have clients who basically have done all the SEO work and all the PPC work and then they're sitting around wondering why their companies are not growing very quickly or as quickly as they should be. There's a cost involved with that free internet and the cost is that the cost is time and time is money. If you're sitting around and waiting for people to find you and doing everything you can to be findable on the web you're going to get a tiny fraction of the attention and the readership and response you would get if you can go into the mail. And so a lot of times even paying four or five hundred dollars or six hundred dollars a thousand to go into the mail is the answer if it's going to bring you that business faster. So cost efficiency is more than just well how can I do this on the cheap. It's a calculation that includes the revenue and the profits that you can generate by being more proactive for example. A good example would be a company is sitting here and they're making 100 sales a month right now using PPC and SEO. But there's a mailing list out there and of course because they have no media costs or profit margin is very high on those sales. But there's a mailing list out there full of people who are just like his prospects who bought products like this before who purchased with credit cards who bought as a result of a direct mailing. And if he would just mail that list now he could get hundreds more or even thousands of orders and although the margin on each order would be smaller he'd end up making a lot more money. OK well next is after you've identified who your prospect is and how you're going to reach him you might you need also to know what is his most pressing problem or desire and relative to the subject area that you're going into because that's what will activate people is it's what's going to stop stop them in their tracks if you have it in the headline. It's what's going to cause them to read your sales message and it's it you know connecting with that problem or desire is going to be what gets them to ultimately click your order button or or call your toll free number or return your response device. So identifying what it what it is is motivating him now in the financial markets right now there are two things going on at the top of the news. OK we know that people are worried about the economic slowdown in the recession and how bad it can get right. But we also know that gold just hit a new all time record high today oil copper platinum palladium are all skyrocketing and commodity investors are getting rich. So your your prospect could have two completely opposite emotions right now. He can be terrified that he's going to lose his job and the economy is going to crash in the stock market is going to crash. But at the same time he's well aware because it's at the top of every financial newscast that gold is going through the roof. And so he's he's feeling greedy about that and he's feeling fear about the state of the economy. So in fact I was just having this conversation with a client this morning who's trying to decide what is the next product he wants to promote in this environment. Does he want to go with a product that that relieves people of their fear or does he want to give people a product that allows him to harness a hot trend in this market. And we defaulted to going for the greed in this situation. There'll be a time in the near future where the fear is overwhelming when we see the stock market fall seven eight nine hundred points in a day. But for the time being right now the stock market is kind of going sideways. The really dynamic movement is in this other area. So you need to you need to decide you need to figure out what the most most powerful motivating emotion is going to be in that prospect. Then you need to decide what you can deliver that'll solve that problem or fulfill that desire. This is about bringing value to people's lives. As we'll see in a moment the worst mistake you can make as an information marketer is to sell something on the front end. Bring up new customer in selling him something that just doesn't really bring value. There are a lot of people who do this. They write really red hot promotions for a product whether it's an information product or you know some other product. But then when you get the product the it disappoints. See the biggest risk the biggest cost both in terms of time and money and the biggest risk is that new customer acquisition new customer acquisition promotion. That's the biggest risk you'll ever take when you get into this business. You could go out there and spend money and time developing a promotion and have it fall flat on its face. So you have capital risk as well as risk to time. And if it does fall on its face that means that the time you spent developing that you weren't developing another promotion or another product or another business. So there's lost opportunity cost as well. So the easiest thing to do is that once a customer is online once you have a customer on your database who has already bought from you likes you and had a good experience with your product the easiest thing in the world to do is sell to him. That's why when you go to a direct mail for example promotion and you're trying to acquire new customers for a hundred dollar product you'll probably be pretty happy with a half a percent response rate because you can break even at that level. But when you're selling to a customer who's already bought from you a half a percent would be a disaster. You'd expect three, four, five, ten times that much. On house file promotions I've seen as high as a ten percent response rate that's twenty times what you get in the mail when you go out to get a new customer. So that's the value of having those customers on your file. It makes no sense to go out and spend all that money and take all that risk and then deliver a product that will make your customer file unresponsive later because they've already learned that you don't deliver what you promise. So point number four here is you absolutely have to make sure that if anything you over deliver in the product you under promise in the promotion you over deliver in the product. Then you need to ask yourself what product format your prospect is shown he's willing to accept. And this is fairly easy to do. You know if you're doing direct mail you go to the library you get the most current standard rate data service book and you sit down at a table and you look up your niche, your industry, your competitors and you see what those people are buying. Are they buying books? Are they buying special reports? Are they buying cassette series, DVD series, live events? What is it they're buying? They've demonstrated that's a psychographic fact, a psychographic fact about those prospects. They have sent the message, hey I feel comfortable acquiring information in this format. Okay, again an example from the financial newsletter industry. People have made literally billions of dollars selling investment newsletters to rich old white men. That's what Agora and Phillips and Weiss and KCI that's what they do. But the minute they've tried to sell those same newsletters to young people and people younger than 50 they've gotten their heads handed to them. Because people younger than 50 like getting their information in other ways. They don't want to read adult boring black ink on white paper newsletter. They want to read glossy magazines so they get Kiplinger or they get Forbes or Fortune or another magazine or maybe they get it online. So understanding the product's format that your prospect prefers is very important because he is telling you this is how I like to absorb my information. Number six, can you achieve your objective in a single installment or would a series be more effective? This gets back to the product again. You need to decide whether your topic is a book or a newsletter. One of the classic squibbos that I see young people make when they first get a job with a financial publisher happens in the meeting when people are sitting around and brainstorming new product ideas. Nine times out of ten the youngest, newest person in the room will either suggest that we do an investment newsletter for women or that we do an investment newsletter on some subject that doesn't change with time. The reason why an investment newsletter for women is not a brilliant idea is that women invest in the same stocks that men do and so they need the same information men do. So nobody has to to date fielded as successful although many people have tried fielded as successful investment newsletter just for women. Again, when I'm in those meetings I just simply say, okay, well I tell you what, we'll do this, just tell me how investing is different for a woman than for a man and then if you can do that then I think we should do this. It all comes down to the same decisions. They have to make, they may feel differently about things but the bottom line is that a good stock is a good stock, a bad stock is a bad stock and that's pretty much it. But a lot of times also the second mistake is that they'll come in with an idea that would make for a really great book because the information that's going to be presented doesn't need to be updated every day or every month. The topic is static so if that's the case you want probably a static product. If the information is dynamic, changing all the time, if your advice or the techniques used to solve the problem or achieve the desired result change as time goes on then you may want some kind of a continuing product. And of course if you're trying to get a person from point A to point B in terms of their understanding of a subject then maybe it's a course. Maybe it's a course on investment or on, you know, I saw an interesting show on television last night, my daughter's really into horses and there's this guy named Pirelli and he goes around the country showing people, he's kind of like the dog whisperer only for horses and he has courses where he teaches you to improve your horses' behavior that sell for thousands and thousands of dollars. So, you know, that's not, that could be a book. It's probably not a newsletter because, you know, the dynamics don't change that much from month to month. You can deliver all of his information at one time. So it's either a book or if the information is so exhaustive that it can't be absorbed in a few hundred pages in a book then maybe it's a course. And then finally what is he willing to pay? And then this is again the idea of, you know, market research. Go grab that SRDS or go to the website so you're going to be competing against or whose prospects are similar to yours. And just open up an Excel spreadsheet and start putting in there. What kind of products are they buying? What are they charging for them? Are they books, newsletters, magazines, e-zines, courses? How much do they charge? Are they continuity programs or are they cash up front programs? How do they work? And as you work your way through that process and you already know what niche or basically what you want to write about, your product will start taking shape in your mind. Now there's some classic errors. I mentioned the classic error that Paris made when he attempted to become an information publisher many years ago. But the classic errors are that, you know, well, you get all excited about your information product. Oh man, this is going to be great. And you develop the product then you go out there and you start promoting it. And sure enough, you're bringing in new customers that are profit. But not much of a profit because it costs you money to bring in new customers. Okay. And the thing then runs its course and you're sitting there when the dust settles with the final realization that you made very little money at the end of the day for all of that work. And so instead of looking at an information product as an opportunity to make a whole lot of money, think of it as the beginning of a business and the beginning of a relationship with the new customers that you're going to generate. And the three things that I have on the screen right now are things that you need to think about. All right. Let's say we're doing this online. All right. And I've just read your sales letter and I love what you've got to offer and I decide to buy it. So I click order now and I go to your sales page. Right. Now on that sales page, I'm going to fill out my credit card application information, all that good stuff. Now on that sales page, you could have a force free trial on there, a little box with a check mark and it could be prechecked if you want. This says please also include a free three month subscription to blank blank blank. I understand if I like it, I'll do nothing and you'll invoice me at the rate of X otherwise I'll cancel it my first 90 days and I'm nothing. All right. That's free money. The guy's already ordering. This is a moment in time when he's happiest with you, most excited about his product, having a force free trial for a secondary product is a great way to go there. He clicks send, clicks order. Okay. He's finished with the order form, clicks order. Most people will just throw up a thank you page. Why not instead present him with another opportunity to buy? You've got his credit card information and a script can be written so that you offer him a second product. Maybe it's the same product again for a friend. Maybe it's the advanced version of this product. Maybe it's something else and all he has to do is click a button on that page and he's automatically built for that product as well. Then at the very least, the third item here is have a secondary product ready to go. Even if you have a bump, okay, even if you have a force free trial, both of these happen at the time of ordering, right? Well, now you've got a customer and what are you going to do with him next month? This is the importance of thinking of this as a business and not as just a one shot deal. It takes a little extra time to have a bump product and a force free trial product and they have a secondary product that you're going to offer them next month. But think about yourself down the line. You've got a thousand or 2,000 new customers and then there you say, your face hanging out. What are you going to do with them? You go through this process and now you're forming a business and a very profitable one too if you go about it the right way. I hope that helped. In slow economic times, people are going to be looking for more ways to make money. What are the key psychological triggers you hit in down the economic times? In times like this, all of these things are happening at the same time in all of us. Right now, yesterday we got some bad job numbers, we got some bad inflation numbers, we got some bad earnings numbers, we got some good ones too. But there's a feeling of fear, what's going to happen? And you know what? Every one of us does the worst case scenario thing. Now, maybe it's when we're just falling asleep at night or we're not otherwise engaged, but we begin playing with what's the worst that could happen because of this situation. And we play this movie in our minds of the absolute worst that can happen. And all of us are doing that all the time. We're doing that with relationships, we're doing that with business, we're doing that with investment, we're doing that with our jobs. And so fear is constantly present. It's more active and more compelling, more motivating at a time when we're taken out of that, oh no, what if, and put into the now, the present time, when something really bad is happening. It's a lot of the economic situation we're facing right now. We're kind of hoping that it won't be as bad as some people say it might be, and it probably won't. And we're certainly hoping that it won't touch us personally. But it wouldn't take much to really activate that fear in terms of bad news in the marketplaces. Second, helplessness. This is a big one. One of the very top reasons or causes of depression and suicide is a feeling that we're not in control of our own destiny. And again, this question was really couched in economic terms. And there are millions of people out there who go to work and work hard all day and have been for decades. And their immediate future is not within their control. If the economy hammers their company particularly hard, or if their company is not well managed and prepared for a time like this, they could lose their jobs. And they have no control over that. And so helplessness is a big motivator. We all hate that feeling. Third is resentment or anger. And right now it's an election season, so it's really easy to find some people to be mad at, because we're thinking about politicians anyway. But I mean, frankly, and you're not going to hear me say a lot of bad things about the president, but the administration in Washington is responsible for what is happening now in the real estate market. There's absolutely no doubt about that. The administration lowered interest rates, making houses extremely cheap to buy, which drove up demand and caused the supply to drop, and therefore caused prices to rise. And at the same time did not regulate the lending industry and allowed all kinds of abuses to happen where people were allowed to buy more home than they could afford, just so that the banks could make more money. Now, this was a mistake. If you want to be mad at somebody over this, you know who to be mad at. Okay. At a time like this, there's also a desire for security. You know, we're afraid. We feel unsettled. We know that just about anything that could happen. And at a time like this, you start daydreaming about what it would be like to never have to worry about money again. And then number five is really an offshoot of that greed. Hey, if I had a million dollars in the bank, I wouldn't have to be feeling fearful, helpless, resentful and angry, you know, you know, and so the solution to my situation is to become independently wealthy. So depending on what your product is, you know, hopefully if you have a product that helps people make money, you know, it connects with all of these. And the purpose of testing and direct mail in online really is to find out which kind of a lead is going to get you the greatest readership. And so, but anyway, I hope that answers the question. These are the five things that you need to be hitting on right now very hard. By the way, this resentment and anger thing is very, it's like a two-edged sword. And the financial markets right now, most of our prospects, as I said, are conservative, older men. And so going to them and reminding them of how we got into this mess is not going to make you any friends. So, you know, if that's your market, going with, you know, the White House got us into this, you know, they can't get us out of it. It's just going to basically get you killed in the mail. So, be sensitive as to who you're talking to when you use all of these things. We had a thing recently where we began hiring some copywriters. And one of the things that we said in our ad was that you must be motivated by money. And so, as it's to prove that they had read the ad, when they filled out the application that we had, there was a question on there. What really angers you? And a bunch of the people who filled out this application said, greed. Obviously, they hadn't read the ad. I want greedy people. I want people who want to make money and are willing to work for it, you know. Understanding who you're writing to is very important. Several good questions here. We can move through them quickly. Would it be better to position yourself in B2C or B2B markets right now? It all depends on what the B2C market is and what the B2B market is. If I was working in the business to business markets right now, I'd be wanting to work for companies that do extremely well with inflation as taking off and when the economy is slowing down. Who makes money at a time like that? Well, gold miners make money. Rare coin dealers make money. Pawn shops make money. People that sell cosmetics and low-cost personal luxury items make money. Stores that sell discount products make money. Those are the B2B markets I would want to be in. Obviously, it wouldn't be a store. In that case, it would be like a wholesale or whatever. Companies that sell products that save people money on essential activities. Things that they have to do even during a recession, like eat. Okay, so there are areas of the B2B markets that are going to be extremely good and those are the ones I'd want to be in. The same is true in B2C though, you know, business to consumer. I mean, if you're in a, let's say you're working with a company that sells Rolls Royces, you're probably going to do okay because people who can afford Rolls Royces and good times can also afford them and bad times, for the most part. If you're selling vacations, family vacations to middle-class people who are terrified of losing their jobs, you're probably not going to do real well. So think about the times. Think about where we are right now and select clients or go after clients on the basis of how well you think they're likely to fare as the economy slows. Marcus also is planning to come here and want some advice on whether you should go into English-speaking markets now. And the answer is yes, but you know, don't give up your German and Swiss clients. Just add a few English-speaking markets, add a client or two from the US or Australia or whatever now, and build up your sample portfolio and kind of ease into this. You've got some time and you can have the best both worlds here in a really good position. Number three is a really good question. He says, are we going towards times where long sales letters with lots of non-hype information and valuable background are going to pull best, especially outside the newsletter world, for example, in the venture capital private equity world? I got to tell you, Marcus, I kind of felt like this was a trick question. I know you're not trying to trick me, but you see in the venture capital and private equity world, you're dealing with regulated industries and you're limited in the first place in terms of hype and what you can say. And if your client is doing this correctly, he's got legal counsel and I don't know what the rules are in Germany where you are. But over here anyway, we have a couple of organizations plus our own compliance officers and attorneys who keep us from getting too hypey. So in that sense and in this particular area of venture capital, private placement, private equity, you have to be very cautious in terms of what you say and make sure that you have a good attorney reviewing all your material. But really, I think the spirit of the question has to do with, okay, do advertorials, are advertorials going to continue selling wealth and outpoll other types of promotions? And again, there's no real way to answer that across all industries, all media, all product groups. But essentially, my answer would be we've always been in that time. Since the 1970s, when I started writing sales copy, all of our promotions always delivered value to the reader. And rather than thinking of it as non-hyped information, the idea was to deliver something and answer that the reader is looking for at the beginning of your promotion and then have your promotion salted with more helpful information or advice. Okay? And that keeps them reading and it demonstrates your expertise. So it's a fantastic technique to use. As I said, I've been using it since the 70s. We call them advertorials and the 24-page magilocks that you see in our swipe files and so forth are good examples of those. So yes, and especially online today, if you are familiar with any of the big online gurus, people like Jeff Walker or Rich Sheffrin, one of the mainstays, one of the key points that they're trying to get people to understand is that when you're selling to consumers today, there are three kinds of recommendations, okay? Three kinds of things that are going to push them to buy from you, okay? Number one is third-party proof, okay? That's where you go to that prospect and you say, okay, you don't know me from Adam, but I got a great product and here's all the things it does and here buy it now, right? And then there's when a friend comes to him and says on a blog or forum, a social networking site, says, hey, have you seen Fred's product? It's really great. I've got one. It's working great. Now, you know, which of those two appeals is going to really work the best? Sure, the basic sales letter where you go out and say, here's my product buy it now is always going to work. But anytime that you can have someone else recommend your product for you, that's going to work better because they trust you're a marketer, right? You know how huge the do not call lists are? You know how we all hate marketers in our own lives? You know what happens at my house when somebody calls, a telemarketer calls late at night? I always let the redhead handle them and it's not a pretty sight to watch. You know, marketers are not desirable guests in most people's homes. And so they try to avoid what we sent them. They try not to read it. They try to avoid us at every point. And then when we do get through to them, they're very skeptical about everything we say to them. So, you know, wouldn't it be better to have somebody else appear a friend, you know, doing the recommending for you? So that's a superior way to just going out and saying buy my product. Another way to do it is to become that friend. And the way that you do that is by providing information that has great value to your prospect for some period of time prior to allowing him to buy anything. So in that case, you become the friend. So, yes, advertorials are extremely powerful. And just make sure that you're not just giving them non-hype information. Make sure that you're meeting a need in their life with your promotion. And online, especially, feel free to do it in more than one step. You could take a week or two weeks or three weeks or four weeks from the first time someone comes to you and do nothing but shower them with content that proves your expertise, that proves that you're a friend, that proves that you're their advocate, that proves that you have their best interests at heart. Then when you finally get to the point where you do have a product to sell, you don't have to hype it. You don't have to make unrealistic claims. You don't need to use a lot of exclamation points, you know, slammers all over the place. You know, all you have to do is say, hey, you know, I know you're interested in this and I've got this thing, I've just developed it, does that. And, you know, why don't you take a look at it, click here. It can be very simple then. So what you're doing is serializing a sales letter or an advertorial in that sense, where instead of delivering the whole thing in 24 pages through the mail, you're delivering a lot of little just editorial things that deliver content over time and then allowing them to purchase. It's a great way to sell and it's working better than just pretty much everything online right now. The average subscriber to the total package spends $5 every month with us. Okay? Now, you have some idea what the ratio is of editorial content to promotion because you all are members of the total package. You know that every single day there's a long form value added article that goes to people. Every single day, five days a week. And then we have short emails that go out from time to time when we have a new product that comes up. Now, the ratio of promotion to value added information is very important. And if the ratio is right, maybe you'll get to $5 per subscriber per month. So far at the last time I checked none of our competitors are anywhere close. And we take great pride in that because to us that doesn't say, hey, now we write great promotional copy. To us that says we write a great daily easing that brings value to people's lives. And that's why they know that we know what we're talking about. And that's why they know that their interests are our priority. And that's how they know that we can be trusted. So stick with that concept and mark issue on the right track. Okay, Steve wants to know, can you point me to some good resources on positioning? Look, I've read one book on positioning. It was all I needed. It's made me millions. It was positioning the battle for your mind by Al Reese, Jack Trout, Amazon Hazard. I strongly recommend it. Any of those of you who are not copyrighters or business owners and who aren't interested in positioning, my suggestion is that you get interested in getting a book and read it because it's made me millions. It has. If you were a newbie copywriter, what letter would you send out to get people interested? Steve, that's a question that would require a lot more time than we have today. But essentially, what I used to do, and this is very quickly in a nutshell, I did an entire product on this, the six-figure copywriting course that we have in our store, where I lay it all out, that my process was simply that I wanted to write for investment newsletters. So I get an Oxbridge directory, which is a list of all of the newsletters in the country. And I went through it, and every week I would mail 100 letters. And I went through it first and identified the companies I wanted to work with most. And I put them in an order that I wanted to contact them. Then every week, four times a month, I would send out 100 letters to the companies on those lists working from the top to the bottom. And then in every one of those letters, I would just say, Hi, I'm Clayton Makepeace. I write junk mail, direct mail. And here's what I've done. And here's a couple of samples of my work. And I'm opening my schedule right now for the second half of the year. And if you'd like to talk to me about an assignment, please give me a call. And then down in the PS, I'd say, if I don't hear from you by Wednesday, I'll give you a call. And there are all kinds of little permutations and tricks and stunts that I added to that, but that was a basic thing. And then I got myself into the discipline of every morning when I got up, I would start banging the phones and calling those 100 people. Maybe it was 20 a day, 25 a day, whatever I could do, but I would call those 100 people that I'd mailed to the preceding week. I'd call them this week. And that was enough to get me started. And those jobs, all of this is like 1980, you know? And those jobs then led to, as they started to produce some winners for those people, where the mouth got around, hey, who wrote this piece of Johnny Johnson's mailing? Who wrote this piece of SRC's mailing? And slowly but surely word got out that it was Clayton. And one of the funnest things that happened was that, you know, there was a company I wanted to write for, and they didn't know me from Adam, but I had a promotion in their niche that was working quite well. And so I just sent him a letter where I literally hand wrote, my name's Clayton, I wrote this, and I'd like to create a package for you in November. Give me a call and question mark. And the thing was delivered, and the woman that received it called me on the phone immediately and said, we just had an entire meeting yesterday trying to figure out who wrote this thing. You're hired. So as you get some winners out there, you're, everyone in your marketplace will see those winners. And it's a lot of fun then when you can deliver a copy and say, hi, I wrote this. I got a slide open in three or four months, you know, sent me 12 grand, it's yours. And it's fun to watch. You know what, there are a lot of people who sit around and they think, wow, I just wish I was where Clayton was, you know, making a quarter million, a half a million dollars a month, you know, the money's rolling in, he's doing great. You know what, there are times, especially times like right now when I'm thinking about, you know, how was when I was getting started that I envy where you guys are. Because you're at a point where the rewards, the emotional rewards, as well as the financial rewards, if you do this, if you stick with it, if you make it happen, are just off the charts. You know, once you're established, once things are going along pretty well, you know, it's not nearly as exciting. It's so much fun when you get those first few jobs and when you start getting that recognition and your name starts getting around, it's really a great time. And Mark my words, every one of you that does stick with it and do this and persevere, every single one of you is going to be where I am one day and you're going to be sitting here thinking, I mean, you're going to be as old as me. And you're going to be sitting here thinking, wow, that was fun. Those are good times. Make these was right. You know, that was the best. Okay, Steve also wants to know, what do I do when I get stuck or hit a wall? I quit. I just quit. I get up from my desk. I walk away. I watch TV. I play a video game. I chat with the kids or the wife. Take the motorcycle out for a ride. Get in the portion. I'm a big believer in allowing my brain to solve the problems. And so often that I'll be up against an intractable problem. Sometimes I just have to walk across the room, you know, just get up, go to the bathroom, go pour myself a cup of coffee, something like that. But disengage mentally. You know, you're not thinking about it. You're not worrying about it. When you're thinking about something and trying to solve a problem, your conscious mind is getting in the way. The subconscious is all about making connections. You know, that's where creativity comes from. So, you know, get your conscious mind out of the way. Think about something else. Thomas Edison used to like to take maps. But disengage. That's the best thing I could tell you for when you hit a wall. How many times do I hit it at my work? I do it a lot. I'm working on a letter right now. I've been working on it for three weeks in my spare time. I've probably gone through five drafts, five complete drafts. But I work on it for a while, and then I set aside for a couple of days and I come back to it. The answer to this, Steve, is just that it all depends on the project. There's some projects where I set down. I cranked it out in a day. I'm going to 24-page maglock, long copy in a day. It worked great. There are other times when I went through 20 or 25 drafts, I just couldn't get it. I just wasn't happy with it. It wasn't smooth. It didn't flow. It didn't feel like it was really resonating on a cut level with my prospects. Take the time to do it. I know all of the other top writers I know are like this. Jerry Bentz, Venka, most notably, client calls, when will it be ready? The answer is when it's ready. I saw a great documentary on Michelangelo the other day. For those of you who know the story, I want to give you a heads up. I will screw this up. The Pope had told him he wanted him to paint the Sistine Chapel seal. Michelangelo says, What are you nuts? He says, I'm a sculptor. I'm not a painter. He says, No, I'm not going to do it. The Pope says, Yes, you are. Michelangelo says, No, I'm not. The Pope says, Yes, you are. Eventually, he had no choice. He had to paint the damn sealings. He gets up there and five and a half years into this and he hates it. He hates it. He's a sculptor. He wants to get back to marble. On top of all of that, the Pope is coming in three times a week and said, When's it going to be done? When's it going to be done for five and a half years? Finally, Michelangelo just gets ticked off and tells the Pope where he can stick the entire project. He says, I'm going back to Florence. The Pope then had to go to Michelangelo and say, Oh, please, Mr. Michelangelo, don't quit the project. I am so sorry. Please forgive me. He begged him to come back to do the work. I just thought that was really instructive. I'll mark it up to another little side tangent there. It'll be done when it's done. The Pope had listened to that and believed it in the first place. He wouldn't have to apologize later. Okay, who are my mentors now in the past? The world's changed a lot since I got started. We didn't have mentors and protégés back there in the 70s. When I started doing this, basically, it was up to you. You had to study what other people were doing, what other people were mailing. There were a few books, the Masters, that everyone recommends, Hopkins Cables, OVB. That was pretty much it. I had no mentors. I was lucky enough to have a friend named Michelangelo. You might maybe call a mentor. He had been studying copywriting for some time. He was a friend of the owner of the agency that I got my first copywriting job at. He was one that handed me cables in Hopkins and the Masters. Other than that, pretty much everything I learned was either by trial and error or by studying what successful companies were mailing. The same is true today. Honestly, there's nobody that I work with or that works with me. It's not that I don't think mentors are a great thing. I love to be a mentor to other people, but it's not within me. I just was raised in this mentoring environment. I'm sorry I can't help you more with that one. The key is, I said a million times, it's old news, but get on a lot of mailing lists and read what the great copywriters are writing. You know, Boardroom, Phillips, Agora, KCI, Rodale, read what the great copywriter is. Those guys are the jet fighter pilots of our industry, people who are writing for those companies. It's not like, oh, the Internet Guru who says he wrote a promotion for himself and it pulled really well. The other part of that statement is, yeah, but if I had tested that, what I wrote against somebody who really knew what they were doing, they would have probably kicked the living daylights out of me in terms of response. So the jet pilots of our industry are the ones who go up against each other day after day after day, scrapping for a half a point edge in response or a five or ten dollar advantage in average sale. Those are the people who live and die by their work in a highly competitive environment. So when you subscribe to something Boardroom does or buy one of Rodale's books and start receiving that material, it's solid gold because those are the people who are at the very top of their craft. Those are the best mentors you can have. When I was talking to Gary, not too long, Gary Vincenang, I'm not too long ago, I told him, you know, really you're the only mentor I ever had. You didn't know it because you had no idea who I was, but I was about ten years younger than him. And so, you know, he was already going on and taking the clients that I was dreaming about and producing great winners. And so, now whenever I got a Bence Venger promotion in the mail, I'd sit down and study it, tear it apart, cut it up, and analyze it every single way possible. So it's a great way to learn. What would be the first step in increasing average customer value? Phillip wants to know. Alright, so Phillip's gone into a company and or he owns a company and he says, wow, I want to increase customer lifetime value. Well, there are three components, okay? Order frequency, how often a customer orders from you. Okay? The average order amount, how much he spends each time he buys, and how long he continues buying from you. Right? So those are the three things that determine lifetime value. If you increase, improve each one of these numbers incrementally, okay, the total value of that customer rises in order of magnitude. So order frequency, well, what does that have to do with? Well, I mean, how can I increase order frequency? Well, you can look at the number of promotions a customer receives per month. You can look at what happens in his other contacts with the company, what happens when a product's delivered, what kind of sales material is in the package, what happens when he calls customer service, how are they scripted to either increase his order size or to get another order from him. What happens if he receives a bill from the company is what kind of stuff is in the bill offering, making an offer to him. How can you use discount coupons? How can you use some kind of a membership program that gives him credit for the number of times he orders? What can you do in those areas? There's a whole bunch of ideas that we're going to be looking at. Okay, the next one is the average order amount. And this gets to offer structure. In other words, a client has a health product. You can order a three-month supply, a six-month supply, or a one-year supply. Okay, so what do you want to do here? Well, you want to get everybody to buy the one year, but they won't. So people buy a three-month supply. What happens next? You bump them on the phone with a letter that goes out after the purchase or package stuff that gives them another 15 days to still get the six-month price. If they up their order now, there are a lot of ways to do that. The more you look at the business's processes, the more opportunities you're going to see to increase that average order amount. And then finally, longevity. This has to do with customer retention. And I would start with customer service. A lot of people, when they think about retention, they think, oh, renewal programs. We want you back programs for expiring customers and so forth. But instead, take a look at customer service and how they handle cancels. How are they handling complaints? See, there's an essential truth about this whole business-building thing. And that is that every time there's a contact with a customer, there's an opportunity for a sale. Every time. And yet, people are running great companies and are getting high volumes of calls into customer service asking questions. They're not all negative calls. Some people are asking questions. They want to know when their product will get there. How do I use this? Before I order, I'd like to know this or the other thing. And they just say, okay, we'll answer the questions. That kind of stuff drives me nuts. That's an opportunity to increase the order amount or to make another sale. But then, on the negative side, when somebody's calling in to cancel, that's a great opportunity to make a friend for life, to make sure that customer will come back to you forever. The old thing is that you should be willing to spend, and the cheap guys out there will say, you should be willing to spend as much to keep the customers you originally spent to get him. I think you should be willing to spend five times more. Because you're not keeping that customer just for this month or this purchase. You're keeping that customer for the next year, five years, seven years, ten years. And over that period of time, he's going to spend a lot of money with you if, when it comes to the nut cutting, when the rubber meets the road, if you were there for him, he's going to be there for you. And he'll be with you a long time. So longevity is really big. We're going to do a lot more on that, too, in the weeks ahead. Okay, I hope that helped. All right, we're all the way up to 33. We've got three more. Okay, Stuart is writing an advertorial. And he's got a nice front end on the thing that he's giving away advice. He's giving away secrets on the front end of this advertorial. Do this, do that to achieve the required result or the desired result. He says, okay, now I'm at the point where I've just proven that I know what I'm talking about by bringing value to their lives. How do I transition in the copy to, so by my product? And the answer, Stuart, is it's really pretty simple. So long as you haven't kind of started out in the wrong direction, the tips that you're giving need to be tips from either a premium or from the product itself. And we're assuming an information product here. So your transition is simple. People are always asking me, where in the world do you come up with this stuff? These tips are great. Everything I've just told you was taken directly from the pages of XYZ. Typically, I like my first step after giving them a bunch of mind-blowing information to have it come from a freebie. So that gives me the opportunity to give them more tips and say, and that's also from your free copy of this. And then to provide a whole bunch of bullets that are very tantalizing and really make them want to know the answers to those questions. And that too is from your free copy of XYZ. And then only after I've totally blown them away with the value of the information in their free stuff, then I say, you know, and here's how to get your free copy of XYZ. And then you go into, you know, this great premium or these great premiums are yours free, just for accepting a risk-free trial of my product, whatever it is. So that's a great way to transition it. There are other ways, you know, if you're doing something else where, for example, the tips you're giving them are not from the product itself, you're selling maybe a three-dimensional product, maybe you're selling a garden tiller, but the tips are about gardening, you know, then basically the transition is still into the premium, which gives them more tips about gardening that they get free for trying your product. So that's typically how it's done, Stuart. Hope that helps. Larry, I'm overwhelmed with too much to read. What's the solution? Well, you know, Rich Scherfer and I are buddies, and after spending some time with Rich, see, he thinks I'm a creep because I don't read much. I spend so much time writing, I don't have a lot of time to read, and so he always knows the six principles of this and the four use of that, and, you know, all of these great sounding theories and stuff, and I don't know any of them, but I use them all the time because they're common sense or just do it instinctively. But I tell you what, Rich blows me away. The depth of his knowledge is just humbling, and so I've decided that my solution for this problem, Larry, is that I'm going to go ahead, I'm already a fast reader, but I'm going to take a speed reading course. That's what Rich has done, and the guy is phenomenal. He burns down a big long book in a couple of hours, and I'm going to do that. So that's my solution, okay? Having said that, that isn't where your question is. You want to know how to read fewer of these publications and just the ones that are going to bring the greatest value to you. Besides our own, Ben Savanga Bullets, that's Gary Ben Savanga's newsletter. You can Google all these guys, they'll come right up, okay? Ben Savanga's Bullets, Gary publishes very sporadically, but everything he does is just solid gold, and if I were you, I'd go over to his site. I think it's benavangabullets.com and sign up, and then every time he publishes, you'll know. And then there's Golden Thread, which is AWI's easy, I've enjoyed. Bob Bly is a buddy of mine, and really does a lot of great things on his site. You should check him out. Mike Fort, his copywriter's round table is great. I'm sorry, Jack Fort's copywriter's round table is great. He's one of those guys that's just getting better as he goes along. I really love his stuff. I interviewed the guy who started Copy Blogger last week, wasn't I think? And you talk about a character, you're going to be getting that CD soon. Brilliant, brilliant, brilliant guy, brilliant guy. You should check it out. They have a lot of great articles, fantastic archives. Mike Fort's copywriter's board is also great. A lot of great threads there, and Warrior Forum, of course. And I'm always encouraging you guys to expand your chops beyond copywriting into the whole spectrum of direct marketing, and you'll really see a lot of great threads on Warrior Forum to help you do that. And there are some good copy threads there too. Final question, what new ideas and programs are you working on now or planning for the future? What can we look forward to with excitement? My mission this year is to get as many copywriters as possible, fully educated, direct marketing techniques, so that they have a whole other product line that they can walk into a company with. And one of the problems that's happening right now is that you go through all the training and you become a copywriter, and maybe you write a few pieces, maybe you only have spec assignments. But has anyone taught you when a client gives you a strategy for a piece, has anyone taught you, well, here's how they came up with the strategy? Or has anyone showed you how when you go to meet with a client, you can give him two or three or five ideas that will so blow his mind that he would be, I mean, for his company as a whole, not just for his copy. You know, that he's chanting at the bit to get you on his team. How to spot where the money is falling through the cracks in that company. How to spot the most explosive opportunities on our blog. I told a true story of a friend of mine who called the owner of a small company that had direct response company. They had about 20,000 customers on their file. And gave him one idea and said, if you like the idea, I'll write the promotion for free, all I want is a percentage of what we sell. And we'll send it to your customers. And the client said, okay. And the numbers were just phenomenal. I looked them up when I wrote the thing, but if I remember right, it was $10 million over a weekend they brought in. Okay, now the company owner was more than happy to give away the percentage because it was intangible. It was something out there in the rise and he had never done anything like this before. And he had no idea that he was talking about cutting a check to this copywriter for, you know, millions of dollars. When he said yes. But then when the money came in and was so huge, he had to sit down and write the check. He was thrilled about it because he had four times more money than, or five times more money than the copywriter made from the promotion. So that's the kind of explosive income potential there is when you take a good solid understanding of copy and add it to a good solid understanding of direct marketing technique and opportunities that are buried in every single company out there. So Cheryl says, Cheryl says, Clayton, I rather accidentally happened to stumble upon you in this group. Oh my heavens, I am absolutely loving this and so appreciate you, sincere, sincere, sincere and genuine. Well, thank you Cheryl. We have a lot of fun on these things and I hope you make a lot of money with the ideas that you've heard here. Stuart says, totally off the point, many different market viewpoints. It seems like you have nailed where your markets are right now. While you can't give financial advice, which newsletters would you recommend for us to use? You're talking as an investor, Stuart. I think the best thing to do is to get a variety of views, you know. I think it's important to see the positive and the negative side of what's going on. The bears that I know out there who are saying, oh my God, it's the end of the world, it's the end of the United States, it's an economic power, you know, we're all going to hell, it's going to be terrible, tend to focus on every piece of bad news that comes out and tend to not see so much the 180 million Americans go to work every day to build equity in their bosses' companies or to power the government to add liquidity to the economy. And of course the bulls out there are completely ignoring the negative. And so the best advice I could possibly give is get a balanced view, you know, from all sides and then use Occam's razor. You know, the Occam's razor says 99% of the time the simplest explanation is the best and try to sort out for yourself. Okay, well thank you all very much and this was a lot of fun for me. I hope it was helpful for you. Y'all have a great week. Go out and make some big money. Take care. Bye. I want to welcome everybody for being with us today. We thought it would be a good idea today to directly address questions regarding the partnering model and other things that you guys have been sending us questions on. You know, it's one thing for me to sit around and talk to you all about partnering or copywriting or various things. But to be able to answer your questions directly is really great and I don't think sometimes we take advantage of it enough. So that's what we're going to do today. Y'all have sent in some great questions and let's go ahead and kick it off. Get started. The first one is from Cheryl and Cheryl is evidently in the process of trying to sell the concept of partnering to a client and wants to know how to describe to the client the value that she would bring to that partnership. You know, when you're talking to a client about the idea of partnering, the benefits to the client can be fairly obtuse or fairly vague and very hard to sell unless you first determine what is it the client needs. You know, and that I'm really thinking along the lines of you as the professional marketer looks at a company and says, wow, well, if we just added an easing to the mix or if we added email campaigns or if we added television or radio or direct mail, you know, we could really take this to the moon. So it's your professional opinion of what he needs. Another thing entirely is what the client wants. I have a client, for example, that came to me and said, well, you know, I need to welcome a board series for six of my products and I need more direct mail pieces. We're not testing enough in the mail and I need in-house online marketing campaigns every month. And, you know, he was very clear on what he thought he needed and he needed PPC campaigns and banner and email name acquisition campaigns online. All kinds of things. He had a whole laundry list. And, you know, the thing is if you own a company, you are painfully, you have two competing emotions. Okay, the first one is, you know, you believe in your heart that if everybody in the world knew that you were there, that your company would be absolutely exploding in size because hopefully you think your products are that good. And so the business owner you're talking to has that frustration going all the time. And he's frustrated and probably a little bit amazed that his company isn't growing faster. But the other thing is he is painfully aware of what needs to be done in many cases to get the word out or to bring more customers into the fold. And so he's got this long, long laundry list. And one of the, you know, the great truths of life is, and especially business life, is that there are always more great ideas for promoting a company than there are people to execute them. And that's especially true when it comes to copywriting because, as I say so often, copywriting is the scarce resource. Clients we work with can develop products all day long. They can find people to develop products for them easily. They can hire raw recruits out of college and have them develop products for them. But the scarce resource, the hard person to find is someone who can write great copy to sell those products. So if you as a copywriter who's becoming a rainmaker, who's trying to set up partnerships, can identify first what the client really needs from your professional standpoint, and secondly let him tell you what he wants, then it becomes a fairly easy matter when you're putting together a proposal of showing him what you're going to provide for him every single month in terms of copy, strategy, campaigns, and so forth. And then show him how by entering into a partnership with you a couple of things happen. First of all, he has instant access to a copywriter who can take his company to the moon. To get on board with a good copywriter takes time sometimes, which is one of the problems. Not only is it hard to find good copywriting talent, but to get on the writing schedule of a good writer, you may have to wait a month or two to get a project done. Secondly, a good writer is going to charge you an arm and a leg for every project. As a copywriter or a partner working with a company, you have some security. Because you know that client's going to be with you every month, he's going to be giving you assignments and he's going to be paying you. And that gives you the freedom as a copywriter to give him a break on what you charge for some of these projects. I'll give you a good example of that. For one of my clients, in fact we were just talking about this today, for one of my clients for probably ten years, he has paid my standard fee, which is a $25,000 advance, it's a freelance fee, a $25,000 advance to write a direct mail package, and $50,000 royalty for every piece he mails, and he has to pay me a nickel. And so, you know, we've been doing business that way together for years. And even through the partnership model, I maintained those rates. But recently we restructured our arrangement where the vast majority of my incentive comes from selling things to existing customers. And so, it becomes in my best interest to bring in as many new customers as we can for this client. And, you know, the more I charge him on the front end, the fewer new customers he's going to be able to bring in because he's paying me instead of spending that money to mail direct mail packages and so forth. So, it made sense to me to say, look, I want to give you a huge break on these direct mail packages, okay? I'm not going to charge you a $25,000 advance. I'll charge you $4,000 to write a direct mail piece. That's it. But I still need the $50,000 royalty, okay? So, he's saving $21,000, that's $21,000 advance on every new test panel that he gets from me. I, on the other hand, know that he's going to be more aggressive in the mail, be testing more things, have a better chance of getting big winners in the mail. And then, when these pieces work, then we take the $50,000. And, of course, because I work through an agency, the copywriters I have here at the agency stand to make a chunk of that money. So, you know, that's a long way around to say, when you've got the security of an ongoing client relationship, you have also, and if you're incentivized at a particular point in his marketing process, like I'm incentivized on the back end, there are things that you can do for that client that are going to ultimately make you money that you don't have to charge for at all, or at least not have to charge as much for. So, that's another advantage for a client to partner with you. And the third one is a little harder to sell, but most clients get it right away. Whenever they work with a copywriter, they have to teach that copywriter all about their business. They have to teach them what's worked, what hasn't worked, they have to teach them who their prospect is, the benefits of their product that seem to resonate best with those prospects. There's just a big learning curve that has to happen, and it has to happen every single time a new writer jumps into the mix. If you're a partner, you grow to know your client's company as well as he does, and it's more than just head knowledge. You have the visceral experience of going through successes and failures with them. So, it saves a great deal of time in terms of creating his marketing campaigns. But at the same time, it raises his response because you're working from a position of knowing him and what's worked before. So, those are all great advantages. And there was one more I was going to mention, and it's escaping me now, but if it comes back to me, I come back to it. Bottom line, Cheryl, is my suggestion is, let your client tell you what he needs and what he wants, and then offer to give it to him. And then show him the advantages in terms of time savings, cost savings, and the level of expertise. Oh, yeah, I remember what it was now. The final advantage really is that because you're so intimately involved with his business and you see his response rates over time and see what's working and so forth, you're perfectly suited to be able to come in and suggest new products to promote. So, your role is going to expand from just copywriting now to copywriting and maybe involvement in the graphics process, design and layout process, to product development. And then also, as things develop, you're going to see choke points in his company that are keeping him from quickly testing new concepts or developing new products. And you will find yourself involved in the organizational side as well. The next question is from John. This is more a copywriting question. John is concerned about believability and skepticism. He says, believability is critical to convince the prospect to try or buy the product. He wants to know what steps must be taken to lay the skeptic mind of the potential buyer so that the offer is compelling and they order it. John, one thing I'd like to suggest that you do is to spend some time in our archives because in the Total Package Archives, because I've written at great length about the use of credibility elements in sales copy. And you're absolutely right. Our markets are all becoming more skeptical. Next week's issue of the Total Package is going to be about the most unique direct mail piece I've ever received. It just came in. And on the outer envelope, it's a credit card promotion. On the outer envelope, the return address says, quote, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah. That's it. B-L-A-H, B-L-A-H, you know, that's the return address. And then you open it up and the sales letter says, Dear Clayton, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah. The entire letter is nothing but blah, blah, blah, blah, except there are little islands because there's four things I want to know about your credit card, right? I want to know what the introductory rate is. You know, I want to, you know, there are certain things that I need to know about that card. And so the blah, blah, blahs are interrupted here and there during the letter with big, bold, you know, what the initial interest rate is and with the minimum payment and the other things I need to know about this card. And I thought, you know, I can really empathize with the copywriter who wrote this because, you know, Americans have become so inured to marketing and I think skepticism is part of that, but we've become so tired and so fatigued with it just by the sheer volume of advertising and marketing that we're getting on a daily basis. And three years ago when we started the total package we did a calculation that showed that the average American gets nearly 700 advertising messages directed at him every single day on the web, in the mail, in the newspaper he reads on television that he watches. So he's seeing like 700 advertising messages. And if you are not the average American, if you make a little bit more money or you've expressed interest in a particular hobby or a particular pursuit, you can receive as many as 2,000 to 3,000 advertising messages a day. So just sheer fatigue has us tuning out when it comes to advertising. But also skepticism, especially with the rise of the internet, skepticism is at an all time high. So John, I would suggest that you go to our archives and go through keeping an eye out for articles that we've done. And I mean some of these articles were like 20 pages long in word. So they're very detailed and very how-to and so forth. But let me just cover five ways to allay skepticism and promotions that you may be doing right now. And again there's a lot greater detail on this in the archives. The first thing, I like to use third party sources a lot when quoting facts. Instead of saying the inflation rate is 4%, I want to say according to the US Department of Labor's April report, US inflation rate is running at 4% per year. So by invoking the name of the US Labor Department, I brought instant credibility to the factoid that I just presented. The same is true for example in doing a promotion for a health product. If inactivity is the number one cause of heart disease in America, have the AMA saying it or some other credible source that doesn't have a dog in this fight. Skepticism, and direct mail a lot of it on the web as well, doesn't come so much from the fact that you're offering a product. But it comes from the fact that the facts that you present in your sales copy are self-serving. I mean it's obvious you're presenting a fact in order to sell a product. So every fact you present is suspect. So have a third party source. I have a quote that we picked up a couple of weeks ago where Alan Greenspan says that oil prices will continue to rise forever. So now my client doesn't have to say that. The former chairman of the Federal Reserve said it. And if that's the case then we have a product that could help you profit from that. So third parties. Second, obvious customer testimonials. Excuse me. It still amazes me how many times I see an otherwise good direct mail package that's either extremely light on testimonials from people who have enjoyed the product or maybe even totally devoid of them. So make sure that's on your checklist. Look for opportunities to use testimonials in the running text of your sales letter. Maybe in a call out box on every spread. On the order form. On the premium flyer. You know. And get testimonials if you have a free giveaway or a premium. Don't think that you have to just have testimonials about the product that you're actually selling. You know. Look for solicit. Get testimonials about the product. Your spokesperson. The free gifts that they get along with the offer. The quality of your customer service. You know. Your operators were so friendly and helpful on the phone. About how fast your product was delivered after they ordered it. You know. All of the key points that are involved in this transaction. Look for ways to use another source. A customer testimonial in this case. You know. To drive home the truth. That all of this is indeed superior to what the prospect may have experienced before. Expert testimonials are also great as a copywriter. You know. It takes time. And if you're moving from one project to another. You know. Doing one project a month or every couple of weeks. You know. Getting a client. To go out and solicit testimonials from respected people in this field. Is not always easy to do. But making the suggestion. Even if it's not something that's immediately going to be immediately available to you for the use in your copy today. Well at least. It'll do a couple of things. I'll get him thinking about it. And hopefully he'll actually do it. And secondly. And next time you work with that client. If he's a freelance client. Those testimonials could be available to you. But you know. We. One of the best things that a publisher financial or a health publisher can do for example. Is to have a very active and very experienced public relations team. And that. Getting that team then to have your articles by your. Spokesperson. Placed in the relevant industry publications. Is a great way to go because. They when they present your spokesperson's viewpoint. In their magazine or newspaper or on their TV show. They have to introduce your spokesperson. And in order to introduce the spokesperson they have to give his qualifications. And when they give their his qualifications they're validating his qualifications. And they're giving you a quote that you can use. And if any of you. I know most of you have seen my. Financial promotions. Direct mail that I've done over the years. You'll see in there that I am shameless about the use of that. I mean. If. If. The Washington magazine says that. You know Martin Weiss is America's number one investor safety advocate. That's going to be on every direct mail piece I do. It's instant credibility. Again because it's not self serving it was somebody else. That said that about my spokesperson. Number four. Those of you who have been with me for a while are aware of the. Enhanced or or oculation's direct oculation direct mail pieces. That I did. Starting several years ago with health resources. And the product is. A key leading product called the E.D.T.A. that. Bonds with calcium in the in your arteries and by doing so. Causes the plaque that's in the arteries to let go of the artery wall and to be fleshed away. And so. We. There's a test that you can do where you can actually see. The rent this calcium this road road calcium in urine. And so in our direct mail promotion we actually had this little. Experiment that the pros prospect could do himself at home. When he got the product. You know where where he he took a beaker of urine and held it up in front of a light bulb and. And after taking or oculation for. Forget what it was three or four days. And you could actually see the calcium suspended in the urine. And so that was a proof element that was a demonstration. We've done the same thing. In showing how other health products work by using illustrations that look like they were taken from grace anatomy. But in those demonstrations we would show how. Fibrin in the blood cause causes clotting and how clots can cause. Fluvidus or even a stroke or a heart attack we showed you know. The showing the disease process with a demonstration. Is a very effective way to get past skepticism. And in all of these things to the final thing that number five I'm just going to suggest to you John. Is. Very simple reason. There's there's there's a method that. Really is at the heart. Of everything that we do and I mean I learned how to do it when I was a kid and I was trying to. Convince my parents let me do something. But the way it works is you begin with a common touchstone. You begin with a fact or a point of agreement. A fact that somebody already knows. Okay this is something you don't have to sell. Like you know if you're an investor right now we might say. No secret that the dollar is falling like a rock. Well if the guy's an investor. And he's on the list that we go to when we're trying to sell investment products he knows that so. Okay. Right off the bat we're in agreement on something right. And then you build on that fact if that's true then. If if X is true then why is also true right. And you just bring him along with you one step at a time. You might for example say. Right now oil prices are through the roof. And gold prices. Have moved up right in lockstep with. Well the investors going to say okay. And then you can go to a third party source. And Alan Greenspan says. You know oil prices are going to continue rising forever. Because. Supplies are dropping demand is rising. You know and the dollar is falling in value so it buys less oil. Okay. What does that mean that means that gold is also going to start keep rising forever. So you have a chain of logic. So you know this. Item number five reason really is. Logic. You know if you can build a case. That's so strong that it just blows skeptic skepticism away. Your. Your pretty much home free. So and really that. That technique is the heart of what we do. Of salesmanship in print reasoning with the prospect. Helping him to see. Things from your perspective. And then convincing him. To move to take the recommended action. So I hope that helps John. Be sure to check out the. The archives though. Because you're going to find a lot more on this because it's a big one with me. You're going to see how for example I. I love to use it either a testimonial or some kind of a track record statement. In the eyebrow above my main headline. So that right off the bat the credibility of my spokesperson is established. So anyway some great articles in there I can't recommend them highly enough. Okay question number three is from Chris. Chris has a challenge here. Okay he's got an idea. He thinks it would really help solve major problem for a major fortune 500 company. The company is evidently getting kicked around by competition. Chris has figured out a way to stop the bleeding. Turn the tables on the competition. And really get the company growing again. The second paragraph here is his problem. He doesn't have a big ad agency behind him. And he wants to know is there a way to get compensated for ads he writes. For this fortune 500 company. But which would likely have to be placed by the president ad agency. Okay. And so he's asking for advice. You know how would how would I approach this. Chris you've got a really a major problem here. And it's it's more than just placing the ad. The thing is that these fortune 500 companies have long term exclusive contracts with ad agencies. And some of the bigger companies have several ad agencies. Maybe one for every product line that they have. And if you call any rep at in you know any executive at the fortune 500 company with an idea. All they're going to do is give you the phone number of their ad agency. And now the ad agency has absolutely no interest in your idea. They are first of all they have no idea how to sell products to begin with which is why they're not direct response ad agencies why they're general ad agencies. Secondly, secondly most of them have creative directors graphic artists supervising copywriters. They just don't get it. They don't understand how to sell a product. So you're dealing with the anti Christ you're dealing with with people who who are have have a vested interest in not accepting your idea. Not working with you and who don't really respect what you bring to the table in the first place. So I think you see the direction this is going. You are the better chance of winning the lottery than to have to get a deal with a fortune 500 company. Given those facts what I would do in your situation is if I have an idea. For a company like that rather than going to the fortune 500 company. I would figure out okay what are the mid sized and smaller companies that are competing with this fortune 500 company. You know they could use an idea like this. And then I would approach those guys. The smaller and mid sized companies are far less likely to have the kind of agreements in place that a fortune 500 company will have. The only ray of sunlight I could offer you is that if you had an insider at the company that had the year of the person who makes the advertising decisions. You might be able to get a hearing for your idea. But in most cases the chances of them seriously considering it are fairly small. And the fact of the matter is most of them aren't really to be that trusted anyway. Remember you're dealing with Beenal self interested executives here and quite who are to him stealing great ideas. Kind of the high point of their day. So you know look for the smaller company the smaller more entrepreneurial company the hungry company. Rather than these big bureaucratic bloated fortune 500 firms. You know there's a reason why fortune 500 companies don't grow as fast as companies that are one one hundred their size. And it's not just market saturation. It's the entrepreneurial spirit. The willingness to value new ideas. So all right. Wish I had better news for you Chris. That's pretty much the way things are. OK next one we have Gabrielle from from the UK. Interesting name Gabrielle. Let's see here. OK Gabrielle is looking at moving to Spain or Brazil. And like to know the partnering how the partnering techniques should differ for use in international markets such as UK or Spain. And it goes on to say that the contracts I've presented really wouldn't help in the UK. I have no way to know that Gabrielle. I have to assume that the answer that would be yes. But the the the people in in the UK and Europe that I've dealt with again tend to be highly entrepreneurial firms run by the founders that are just hungry for growth. And even even when they get huge so long as the founder is still at the helm they tend to have that nature. And if they are like that then they're looking for opportunities to speed up the marketing process improve the quality of the marketing and to to you know use the marketing to really grow the company. One company springs to mind is Norman Rentrop's company over in Bonn for like Norman Rentrop. He's a publisher and Norman is a great guy. He's a workaholic. The company is famous for its two o'clock in the morning board meetings. And I can see Norman jumping at a deal like this.
