So I think the principles here because of the benefits to the company are pretty much would I would assume that they're pretty much universal. Going back to the question we just answered though the size of the company is key. And also you know I've seen all of the all of the prime companies in my market niche in the health and the investment niches change over the years. You know I was there when you know Robert Kepphart started KCI and and when Tom Phillips started Phillips Publishing and and Chip Wood started SoundView and Martin Weiss started Weiss Research and Howard Reft started the Reft times. And I saw all of these things from their earliest days and I also saw them years later decades later when the original principles sold the company or retired and you know gave the reins to someone else. And the difference in the aggressiveness and the genius of the of the marketing in those companies changed when the founder left. So it's another thing to look at when you're looking at partnering. Look for a company where the founder is still at the helm and make sure that you're talking with the founder the owner of the company and not a middle management marketing person. Keep in mind that the middle management marketing people have a vested interest in keeping you out because when you come into the company they have to give up some amount of control and prestige and you know opportunities to impress the boss. If you're the guy coming in with all the great ideas and so forth a lot of marketing people are threatened by that and they're going to try to kill the deal. So dealing with the owner of the company is very important. And Gary all as far as the contracts we provided they you're absolutely right. I don't think I've ever even seen a contract that was drawn up in the UK. So you know I'm sure the language is going to be very different but the deal points in those contracts are worth thinking about. So you know we're not trying to we're not trying to make sure that attorneys don't have enough work by giving you those contracts. There you should always go to an attorney when you're putting together any contract that is going to potentially represent a substantial amount of money to you or to a client. So but but the deal the deal points in the contract hopefully will be helpful to you. All right. Rich asked question number five. Suppose a copywriter has already written successful direct mail packages in a catalog for a client but is not getting royalties or percentage on the back end to the house file. Since the client is accustomed to getting a free ride so to speak what would you do now to convince the client to start paying a profit percentage. The main answer that question is if you've written something that has worked well for a client and worked better than what your competition or better than what he's used in the past then you're in the perfect position to simply be unavailable. The. Someone asked me this at the power marketing summit and they said you know how do you raise your rates. How do you just raise your fees. And I said write a winner and then be suddenly unavailable. That's how Gary Bencevenga did it. It's how Jim Rutz did it. Jim. Blows into town writes for three or four promotions and then disappears for a year or two. He's involved in overseas ministries and so he just disappears for a year or two. Then he blows back into town sends an email to three or four of the clients he's worked with in the past and says basically hey I'm in town for six months. I'm going to write four packages and the fee is 100,000 flat cash in advance. If you want on my schedule you better contact me immediately because I just sent this to a whole bunch of people and chances are these four slots are going to be gone fast. And it's friendly. There's no height. It's just true and everyone has seen this pattern over the years. So if you've written something that's worked you're in a cat bird seat and becoming unavailable or becoming at least harder to get to harder to schedule time with. Writing time is just a wonderful position to be in. The beauty of that is quite often you don't have to raise your rates. The client if he's really sold on the quality of your work and the incremental increase in response that you've given him you don't have to raise your rates. The client will raise them for you. So and obviously I've done this a few times in my career. I mean the first promotion I wrote I charged $800 to write a job. I charged $800 to write a direct mail promotion for an investment newsletter in 1979. $800. I was doing it on a typewriter where I had to retype each draft from scratch. And I went from there to the rate that I charge now which is $25,000 and $50 a thousand. And you know the quantities of these mailings can be huge. You know that we were talking earlier about demonstrations to establish credibility. That has gone out now in well over 60 million direct mail pieces. And my royalty on that was 50 bucks a thousand. So it gives you an idea of the incremental increase in wages from you know six or $800 for a direct mail piece. So it's an ongoing process and as you create winners you bring your rates up behind you. And Haroun's being a smart ass. He just put a comment in here. I was one year old when you wrote that first financial package. Thanks for reminding me. So anyway the, I hope I made that point clear. You need to constantly be reviewing what your rates are. Another thing that I found was very helpful was if I wrote you know a winner for client A, I would then go to his competitors and make sure they knew that I wrote that catalog or that direct mail package. Make sure they knew that I wrote it. What then happens is they immediately say, oh well would you write something for us? And then I would engage them in conversation about that. But knowing that I had a better shot at negotiating a higher rate with my client, I would immediately beat feet back to the client and say hey you know what, your biggest competitor over here just called and he wants me to write a direct mail piece for him. That's like fingernails on the chalkboard of a client. And I think they're going to be, you know, it makes them much more willing to negotiate with you. So I hope that helps Rich. But it is a continuing process as you establish more and more winners in your career to constantly be asking yourself what's the next step in terms of compensation, what's the next step. Okay Marcus has already gotten started in partnering. And he's looking at a payday, a potential payday of over, you know, there's a period there but I'm thinking it's 500,000 euros. But he's got some concerns. His partner is good to him and he seems honest, but they're very slow in getting their marketing out. Okay, remember what I said earlier about seeing choke points in your client's business? Well so already Marcus has an opportunity there to show his value to the client by trying to help identify what those choke points are and offering solutions to speed the marketing process. Their list is far smaller than I assumed. And it's part of the learning curve. Yeah, absolutely. Never assume anything. Get counts Marcus. And when you're doing a deal with a new partner. And remember you can keep them honest. You can go, you can find out who their list broker is and get a copy of their data card. And that'll tell you exactly how large their list is. Or you can go to the library and crack open the standard rate and data service for direct mail lists. And if their list is on the market and being rented, most of them are if it's direct marketing company. That will tell you how large their list was at the time that that particular edition of the SRDS was printed. But the size of the file is absolutely crucial. And I have to say, I kind of got caught up in this thing. I had a national organization contact me six, eight months ago and wanted me to take over their marketing. And my assumption was, holy cow, you know, I know this organization. They must have three, four million names, you know. And I asked the question a couple of times, never got a real good answer. And then later on after I expended a lot of effort with the organization, found out that their list was only about 60,000 names. Now, I have to say, you know, if this had been a company that was selling, you know, financial newsletters and they had 60,000 names, I would have been fine with that. I would have been really excited about the back end potential and the commission potential on a list of that size. But this wasn't, this was a national organization that, and you all would know the name of the organization. And I was just dumbfounded when I found out they only had 60,000 names. And of course, because this organization was dealing in much smaller dollar amounts on each transaction, it was kind of a deal killer for me. And I only found out after I'd expended effort. So double checking list size is absolutely essential. But that's kind of a side trip here. Marcus question really is, how long should he keep going with this client? And I'm not really clear about this last part, but over six weeks in work, he's evidently invested more than six weeks in work in this client. Marcus, the, and I think this is good for all of us to keep in mind. You know, the key to partnering is, and I do this when I'm negotiating and signing a partnering contract right at the beginning, is just to say, look, let's, you know, we're going to try this. I'm going to do my best, you're going to do your best. But if at any time it's really not working for either one of us, we need to have another conversation like this and fine tune it. Fine tune this agreement as we move along. Opening that door and having that honest forum so that you can go and say, hey, you know, this part of the deal is working for me, this part over here isn't. And if, you know, one or two things has to happen, you know, either this problem is going to get solved or I'm going to have to change my fee structure to compensate for it. Having the freedom to have that kind of conversation with a client is absolutely essential, especially if you hope to be working with that partner over the long haul. And I've done that numerous times with all of the companies I've partnered with. For example, it sounds to you, sounds to me like you really like the partner. You say he's kind and he's honest, okay? But your frustration is that they're very slow. And this happens a lot, you know, you work your tail off to get something done and then it just languishes with the client rather than getting out there in the marketplace work and start generating money for everybody. So, you know, if that's a problem and it's with the client and the client likes you and you like the client, then you need just call him up and say, you know, I'd like to come to your office and spend a little time with your people and then I'd like to come back to you and talk about how we could speed up the marketing process. You know, a lot of companies haven't really thought through what it costs them when each campaign they do takes a week or two weeks longer than it otherwise could have. And, you know, one way to multiply sales and, you know, sales revenues and profits at a company is, you know, just going in and saying, okay, right now your turnaround time from the, you know, the time that you test a promotion to the time you roll out is eight weeks. You know what? What we're going to do in this meeting is we're going to cut two weeks off of that. And we're going to do that, you know, one day at a time. We're going to go through and we're going to say, well, if we do this a little differently, we can save a day. Over here we could save two days. And everybody's ideas are welcome, but come heller high water by the end of this meeting, we will have cut our turnaround time on our direct mail rollouts by two weeks. Okay? And if you do that and the guy is now doing, say, six mailings a year, you know, and you cut two weeks off of each of those mailings, you've saved 12 weeks, which means he's got at least one and maybe, you know, two additional mailings this year, which means an incremental increase in the number of new customers he's going to get, or if they're house file mailings, revenues he's going to generate. So time is money in a very real way when it comes to marketing. And so if you can go in there and again, Marcus, you know, you can look at this as glasses half full here. If you can go into the client and solve this problem and create a marketing machine for him that really clicks and that, you know, moves this stuff through the pipeline more quickly. So this is generating money more quickly. You've just increased the value that you bring to the table in this partnership and I personally have done this kind of thing for a client and he said, well, how am I going to pay you for this consulting time? And I said, no problem, give me 10% of your company. He goes, okay, so I got stock options for, you know, 10% of the company that were ultimately worth, you know, hundreds and hundreds of thousands of extra dollars for me. The amount of time I invested solving his marketing problems, licking up the process, you know, and everything was probably a few weeks time over a period of three or four years. So it was a good return on investment for me and it made him a fortune too. So look at it as an opportunity. Having said that, you know, if you go to the client and you say, you know, he's costing us both boatload of money and you can't get response from him and at the end of the day you have to do a cost-benefit analysis. You have to look at the client and say, okay, if I had known the things I know about this client now back when I originally set up the partnership, would I have accepted this relationship on these terms? And if the answer is no, then you have two choices. You can either end the relationship or you can change the terms. And once again, it's simply a matter of, you know, laying your cards on the table with a client. Your client understands that you're a businessman, you know, just like he is. He understands risk reward and he understands return on investment. And if your direct, if your promotions are languishing someplace in his shop rather than being used where they can be generating money for the both of you, it's in his interest, not only is it in his interest to solve that problem, he's going to respect you for bringing it up because after all, you're a business owner just like he is. And he can feel your pain. So, you know, sometimes it's hard to be a grown-up on these things and precipitate a crisis, even a mild crisis in somebody's life by saying, you know, something has to change here, but it's always the best way to go. Marcus also had another question and I was going to delete this one, Marcus, but you know, the truth is because you already had one in here, but it's not about partnering and it's just a darn good question. Does it make sense to work on two big projects simultaneously? It seems the good thoughts always come from for project A when you work on project B and vice versa. Marcus, really the answer is each of us has to find the work process that works best for us. And for me personally, there are a few things, a few eternal truths about that work process. I find that on each project that I'm confronted with, my work process changes somewhat. The one truth that seems always to come back to me is that I'm most ready to write a new promotion for a product the minute that I've just completed writing a promotion for that product because I have tons of unused ideas that were great but just didn't work, you know, with the way the promotion, with the original promotion idea. For me personally, working on two big projects simultaneously does not work. I need to be able to really eat, sleep and breathe a project over a period of time to get my best work done on it. But you know, you need to do what works for you. Paraslan Propolis sits down at the beginning of every project and writes 300 or 400 fascinations about the product. I couldn't possibly do that. Carleen sits down with a product, Carleen Angwag Cole sits down with a product, and comes up with, you know, dozens of pages of the craziest, most off-the-wall ideas you've ever seen in your life, stuff that would never work in a million years. But she pursues every single one of those ideas because quite often those ideas will yield other ideas that will work. So, you know, every writer's process is different. My process is different on every single promotion I do. So just find what works for you and go for it. There's no right or wrong way to do this. The only good or bad in this entire process is established at the end of it when you're counting the money. Okay, Rick. What if any changes do you make in your copy if your audience is the officer of a medium-sized company? My son thinks you need to be formal and business-like. I say they all put on their pants the same way. Who's right? There's a principle here, Rick, that extends to your sales copy as well as to proposals. When you're trying to pitch a company for a partnership deal. And that is, you need to respect your prospect's time. We had a copywriting workshop in my offices here last week. I think it was last week. Pretty sure it was, yeah. And the one theme that kept coming up over and over again was, you've got to communicate quickly and efficiently. You've got to get your message across in a way that respects the fact that your prospect, in this case, an officer of a company you want to be doing business with, has extremely limited time and probably limited attention span. So when I was working with Blanchard and Company, we sold the company to a subsidiary of General Electric. And so every single month we had to put together, the head of each division had to put together a report that would ultimately go to GE headquarters and would be reviewed by Jack Welch. And Jack's rule of thumb was that you ought to be able to give him everything he needed to manage the company in less than one page. And I respected that. I thought that was really cool. And he had developed a methodology and knew exactly what he needed, and he would tell him about my division in order for him to be on top of it and to be making the right decisions. So smart businessmen will look at things in that way. How does this apply to your situation? Okay, we're going to get into this next time in great detail. But a lot of the proposals I see, especially, first of all, the people who teach business in college are doing it because they can't do business. I mean, these people don't have a freaking clue, most of them. They've never run a business. All they did was read a damn book sometime in their lives about how to do business. And some other idiot a generation ago gave them an Aeon attest so they got to graduate. Marketing and business professors in most colleges aren't worth the powder it takes to blow them to hell. Okay, if they could do it, they'd be doing it and making millions. They can't do it therefore they're teaching a whole bunch of poor suckers who pay the huge amount of money to go to their stupid class. You know, how to do it wrong. So they're going to show you how to do these massive proposals and to use all kinds of verbose pontifications. Stupid phrases that make no sense like facilitate a synergistic relationship and crap like that. What it comes down to is here's what you need to do. Here's what I'll do for you. Here's what I think will happen when it's all over in the dust settles and we count the money. And here's how I want to be paid for it. Fair enough, sign here. Not fair, fine. You know, I'm going down the road. So keep it simple, get straight to the point. Don't be formal, but be professional. Does that make sense? Formal to me connotes flowery business language and basically bullshit. Forgive me. Don't be formal. Be practical, pragmatic. Demonstrate wisdom by getting straight to the point. And I think you'll be miles ahead of the game. There's a lot more we'll be saying about this next time. I'm getting excited about that our next session because it's going to be a lot of fun looking at that kind of stuff. I'm sorry about my little rant there about professors, but I mean, come on. And by the way, that's why, you know, when when MBAs come out of college, they don't go to work at the top of organizations, you know, they still have to put in their time and learn the ropes and unlearn a lot of the crap they learned in college. So, anyway, all right. That was the editorial, by the way. And it's only fair to tell you I'm a high school dropout, so my my views are certainly not unbiased. Okay, question number nine. Are there certain industries and niches that are more suitable or open minded to partnering? You know, we kind of discussed this a little bit earlier, or at least what my answer is going to be a little bit earlier. One thing it has as much to do with the niche as it does with the size of the company, whether the owner is actually running the company and how committed they are to growth. You find it you find the company meets those criteria. The owner, the guy who's going to make the line show the money is running it. He's because of that. He's eager, maybe even desperate to grow the company and the older he is, the more desperate he is because he wants to retire someday. Either sell the company or put someone else in charge and move on. Okay, so, and the company needs to be of a size where they don't already have lots of prior commitments, especially exclusive commitments with advertising and marketing agencies that would preclude you striking a deal with them. I think those things have a lot more to do with it than what the niche is. I've had arrangements with companies that made desserts and water heaters and sold rare coins and vitamins and all kinds of stuff. It can move across pretty much any niche you can think of. Just make sure that the company is suitable in those other ways. Here's a question that actually came from our staff. We're going to wrap up here in just a second. I'm a brand new copywriter. What's the quickest way to boost my confidence? Make money and get a partnering gig going. Work. You need to work. You need to write every day. You need to emulate things that are working. Haroon's hilarious because he's got the postman pretty much timed here at the office. When that mail doesn't have time to cool off, in the mailbox before Haroon's out there rifling through it to see if there's any good direct mail in there you can study. You've got to be kind. You've got to easily breathe this stuff. That's step one. Step two is create a winner or a series of them. Step three is to convert from... It is a process to go from flat fee writing to royalty writing to partnering. I hope that helps. Let's see what other questions they cooked up for us here. I like this one and we'll end with this one. What are some of the downfalls of the partnering model? If it's so great, why isn't everyone doing it? I honestly don't know. I've been actually tempted to publish my tax returns so that my friends who are freelance copywriters will finally get a clue. The thing is that a lot of them have been to my home and yet they see how we live and they know the incremental difference in income between my model and the way they're doing it with the freelance model. Change is difficult and a lot of these writers have been doing this for 10, 20, 30 years and they've been doing it the same way and they've been moving from client to client and they've been doing the new learning curve with every single project and all of the hassles of freelancing. It's just what they're used to. If they're happy with that, God bless them. Who's to judge them? I think the number one obstacle really is the change isn't easy. As I said earlier, you have to go in and precipitate a crisis of some kind. That's really an overstayment. You have to say to a client that you love and that you've worked with on a particular basis for some period of time, hey look, I want to change our deal. A lot of people are uncomfortable doing that. It's amazing to me but all of these great salesmen in print that I know, that I count as my friends, are really not comfortable selling themselves. To go into a company and they've been comfortable working with a particular way for some period of time and having to demonstrate that they have a lot more value to bring to the table than just good copy is something that they just would rather not do. Frankly, if you're Carline or Paris or one of these other great freelance writers, you're making $800,000, $900,000, maybe $1 million a year anyway. They seem to be pretty happy with the way they're going. In about two to three hours every morning, I've done the work that I'm going to do for the day that makes my income for me. I'm done for the day. The rest of my day has spent doing stuff for the profit center and for other things that don't pay me anything but just to keep me busy. It's actually less work for me over the long haul and more money and so I love it. Having said all that, I just want to say there are some downfalls. Number one, not everybody that you structure a partnering relationship with is going to turn out to be a great client, a great partner. There are going to be problems of chemistry between you and the underlings at the company if you do your deal with the owner. Some underlings are going to resent you. Some will sabotage you. Some will tell outright lies in order to try to turn your client or your partner against you. It does happen. There are going to be times when the market turns against you and your income will fluctuate, but that happens in freelancing too. Let's see. I'm really struggling here to come up with some, to give you a totally honest answer and give you a long list of problems that you can encounter. But like any relationship, not all of them work. Not all freelance relationships work either. And frankly, the benefits outweigh any problems that I could come up with by such a long shot. It's hardly worth struggling over. But anyway, alright guys, I guess I'll take one more. I talked about helping Perastructure his first partnering deal. I did it with Jim Punkrey too and with others. Can you give us more details? Sure. Well, in each case, the copywriter called up and said, hey, I love this partnering model. I'm making great money now, but I think there's an opportunity here. I've got a client that's interested. How do you set them up? So I just ask him, what are you doing for the client now? Where do you see the opportunities? What is it the client wants out of this? All the things that we've talked about on this call. And then we compartmentalize what he's going to do. If he's going to do name acquisition on the front end, we talk about the best way to get paid for that. And we also talk about how are you going to handle the overflow. You know, Perast, for example, brings in copycups. He mentors them. I've met a couple of them and Perast has got a knack for this. The people he's found are just fantastic. So how are you going to handle the overflow when there's more work than you can do? Are you going to do renewal campaigns? If so, how are you going to get paid for those? Are you going to do back-end marketing? If so, what kinds of products does he have? How are you going to get paid for those? How about PPC and online name acquisition? Are you going to be involved in those? Oh, wow, there's not much money in those. How are you going to get paid for those? And see, that's an opportunity to say, okay, hey, look, I'll do your PPC ads for you for free. You know, every morning I'll wake up, I'll check the news, and if there's anything going on out there that has something to do with a product you're selling, you know, in the financial market, for example, I'll check the news. Oh, well, the dollar is down big time today. Okay, so I'll write some more contra-dollar paper click ads, maybe, or something else that I think would fit into the theme of what's going on out there right now. Maybe Obama's pulling ahead in the polls, and so you do a PPC, a bunch of PPC ads on, you know, Obama killed the stock market. Take this poll and find out. I'll do all that for free, because I really want your PPC to be huge, and I won't charge you for that. But I really need you to pay me for this other thing over here. And in order for it to make sense for me to do those for you, and it'll only take me an hour a day, no big deal, but for that to make sense for me, I want to make sure that instead of getting 8% on the back end, I'm getting 10% or, you know, something else like that. So you look at all the different work you're going to do, how to get paid for each. What you can lay on the table is a freebie that the client gets for signing the contract. And then we just kind of worked it all out. And so it's a methodical process of thinking it all through. All right, guys. Well, I hope that helped. Again, thank you all for being with us today. Goodbye.
